category_summary:
Anthropic's **Claude Fable 5** release [dominated the day](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-54939729aa8e). The model—reportedly the same underlying system as the previously withheld **Mythos** but [with added safeguards](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-f8438b88c326)—drew effusive capability praise alongside sharp backlash.

- **Andrej Karpathy**, **Ethan Mollick**, and Anthropic's **Boris Cherny** framed it as a [SOTA step-change](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-cdfeed5c2050), citing long-horizon autonomy ([9+ hours of work](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-35682be24e8d) from a 15-page design doc) and strong self-verification. **Matt Shumer** showcased custom [in-browser ThreeJS 3D worldbuilding](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-74714354ecd5).
- A counter-current of criticism emerged over Anthropic's posture. **Bojan Tunguz** (["Anthropic really takes us for idiots"](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-d02fc1b586fc)) and **Nathan Lambert** argued that [over-triggered safety classifiers](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-63fd2badee2f) and opaque restrictions on open research and open-weight diffusion undermine community trust and safety-wash the launch.
- On tooling, Claude Code lead **Boris Cherny** shipped [nested subagent support](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-8e4713de2a53) and argued [coding is now the easy part](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-8d5f4a07896a) of engineering, with debugging, infra, and product work still ahead.

Beyond Anthropic, **Cohere** launched [North Mini Code](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-73cfe2781215), its first open-source coding model. Other notable threads included **Mayo Clinic**'s [AI detecting pancreatic cancer](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-930b89eb42a9) on routine CT scans up to three years early, and a reported **OpenAI** [confidential US IPO filing](/?date=2026-06-10&category=social#item-b430a8588594).

items.title: This is a super exciting release - Claude Fable 5 is the same underlying model as Mythos but with ad...
items.content:
This is a super exciting release - Claude Fable 5 is the same underlying model as Mythos but with added safeguards. The benchmarks are great and it's SOTA on everything by a margin but I'll add that *qualitatively* also, this is a major-version-bump-deserving step change forward (imo of the same order as Claude 4.5 was in November), peaking especially for long problem-solving sessions on very difficult problems. You can give it a lot more ambitious tasks than what you're used to, the model "gets it" and it will just go, and it's never felt this tempting to stop looking at the code at all (but don't do this in prod!). The model still has quirks that people will run into and the safeguards are configured to be a little too trigger happy for launch, which can hopefully be tuned over time.

I feel a lot of things changing as working software increasingly comes out on a tap. The Jevon's paradox kicks in and I feel my own demand for software growing substantially. You can ask for anything - explainers, visualizers, dashboards, bespoke single-use apps (e.g. a full wandb that is hyper-specific just for your project), you can 10X your test suite, auto-optimize code, run giant research projects with custom HTML for the results, anything! "Free your mind" (Matrix ref). Really looking forward to all the things people build!

items.title: Fable 5 is the biggest step up I’ve felt in our models since Opus 4.5 back in November. After 4.5 ca...
items.content:
Fable 5 is the biggest step up I’ve felt in our models since Opus 4.5 back in November. After 4.5 came out I uninstalled my IDE when I realized that I’d been doing 100% of my coding in a terminal for a few weeks. With Fable, it’s felt like Claude has stepped up from being a coding agent to a thought and design partner in building the product. Fable has judgement, taste, and dimensionality in a way that previous models didn’t, leading me to trust it more with the most complex work.

I think the first time I had this realization was when I asked Fable to debug something. It is the first model I have used that was so methodical and precise, taking measurements and adding logs then verifying that it truly fixed the issue before declaring victory.

There’s nothing in claude code’s prompting telling the model to do that, it’s just part of its personality. It really has this “big model smell” that I haven’t felt before.

items.title: Mayo Clinic researchers have developed an AI model that detects pancreatic cancer on routine CT scan...
items.content:
Mayo Clinic researchers have developed an AI model that detects pancreatic cancer on routine CT scans up to three years before clinical diagnosis.

Published in Gut, the study tested the model, called REDMOD, on nearly 2,000 scans, including pre-diagnostic scans originally read as normal.

REDMOD identified 73% of hidden cancers a median of 475 days before diagnosis, nearly double the rate of expert radiologists.

For scans taken over two years before diagnosis, the AI caught nearly three times as many cases.

The tool analyzes subtle tissue texture patterns, offering hope for one of the deadliest cancers, with five-year survival below 15%.

items.title:
🚨 Fable 5 is something to pay attention to.  

This is another "I had early access to the new Mythos...
items.content:
🚨 Fable 5 is something to pay attention to.  

This is another "I had early access to the new Mythos-class Anthropic model and I want to tell you what I thought of it" post. 

I know, I know, it's annoying. 

But the way I now spend my weekends has completely changed because of this new class of model. 

And I need to share it with you all. 

- This is an actual leap. The jump from "4.8" anything to "5" anything just sounds small, but the functionality shift I felt is big. Within my first few prompts, I went, "Oh, this is it."

- Your work is no longer 9-5. No chance. We have high performing models that can run for 100+ hours. How are you giving complex goal-oriented prompts to these systems? How are you deciding what to kick off? How are you aligning your org on these tasks?

- Reasoning on another level. I hammered the crap out of this model. Fable 5 is the ONLY model to answer a tricky word math problem (MBA-level) I have tested on all previous models. Not only did it get it correct, it verified its own work automatically AND explained where the assumptions might need to change. 

- We will see AI invent. I just cannot imagine a world where it doesn't. It is not a copy-paste system, and I would also argue that human invention can often be a recombination of pre-existing things (iPod), witnessing patterns and best practices across other spaces, and wide experimentation. I've thought this for awhile, this was the first model that slapped me in the face on it. 

- Zero babysitting needed. This was the first Anthropic model that I kicked off, went out to a long lunch with friends, kept my phone open, and didn't have to do squat to steer it while away from my computer. It just...worked.

- Yap forever. I found it handled context compaction well. I didn't once think about the context of the chat and how to manage it and what to do once we hit 1M tokens in the conversation. 

- Still rough on some niche areas like complex 3D (like all models are). Unless you're making straight line things (3D dice/squares/streets), it's not going to crush it out the gate. 

Excited to keep using and hammering it. 

Note: there is now a 30-day retention policy on ALL Mythos-class models for workspaces with zero data retention (ZDR) in Claude Console, use Claude Code with ZDR in Claude Enterprise, or access Claude through AWS Bedrock, Google Cloud Agent Platform, or Microsoft Foundry with ZDR.

items.content:
I want to share some thoughts about the impact of AI on org structure. 

This is based on early conversations with some of the top CHROs in Fortune 500 companies as well as other C-Suite leaders and startups. 

First: why do I think most enterprise org charts look the way they do? Maybe cleaner ownership lines (fiefdoms are fun), maybe easier to serve clients better (ex: the API team at OpenAI shouldn't be separate from the ChatGPT team because the same clients are buying both and don't want to deal with two separate teams), maybe just redundancies and catching errors. 

Layers of people in orgs end up acting like an org sieve or one of those coin sorters. Issues come up, and because multiple people or departments have to review it, many of them are caught ahead of time. 

The structure helps to absorb human mistakes. 

It's a liability support system.

AI-First companies (which tend to be companies trying to reduce friction and create flatter orgs) are pulling out those layers on purpose. 

And as a result, ownership (one person running hundreds or thousands of agents) is getting more concentrated.

If ownership gets MORE concentrated because of layer removal, then that actually RAISES the trust you need in your people, your team, your company. 

When one marketer or finance leader owns an entire outcome, you as the leader have to trust their taste, their risk tolerance, their judgement, their level of "done", etc. a whole lot more because there is no one downstream to fix it. 

And so what happens as a result? The hiring math changes. You're less likely to bring in outsiders into your org (so internal referrals and employees with strong networks become even more important). The CEO is more likely to get back in the weeds and have more conversations with owners. You're more likely to have layers of AI as filters and judges, verifying whether work has been completed to a certain quality level. And I'd argue B players have a harder time hiding and are less likely to be kept. 

AI-first orgs are higher stakes per seat, and I think it has actual implications for hiring, growing, staffing, collaborating, referring, firing, and scaling. 

Change management in enterprises takes years, so we'll see this play out slowly over the next 18 months.

items.title: Most RL for language models pushes a model toward the single answer that scores highest on some rewa...
items.content:
Most RL for language models pushes a model toward the single answer that scores highest on some reward, and a known side effect is that the model's outputs become nearly identical to each other after training; this matters because language models are now often run inside a search loop, where the system generates many candidate answers to the same problem and a cheap checker (running a program's test cases, verifying a final numeric result) keeps the best one.

This works because for hard problems a single attempt often fails, but sampling many attempts and checking each one reliably finds a correct attempt, and checking is far cheaper than generating, so the expensive generation step can be run many times in parallel.

Such a loop only helps if the candidates actually differ from each other, which is the problem the side effect above creates: you pay to generate many answers but get the variety of roughly one.

The authors of this paper notice that rewards in real tasks are usually made of several separate parts rather than one number (for code, whether each individual test case passes; for multi-step question answering, whether each step is correct), and they use this structure: during training they repeatedly draw a random set of weights that combine those parts into a single score in different ways, ask the model to produce several answers in one pass, and reward the model based on how well its best answer does under each random weighting, which pressures the different answers to specialize toward different trade-offs instead of collapsing together.

Their method, Vector Policy Optimization, swaps directly into a standard training algorithm (GRPO) by changing only how the reward is computed, and across four tasks plus a coding benchmark it matches or beats the usual approach once you let the search loop draw more candidates, even solving hard programming problems that the standard model couldn't solve. 

https://t.co/3mmBqMJZv8
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes an RL paper noting that reward-maximizing post-training collapses model output diversity, which undermines best-of-N search loops that rely on varied candidates checked cheaply, and that real-task rewards are often messier.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical explainer of a relevant RL post-training and diversity-collapse problem from a credible author; clear value despite modest engagement.
items.summary_html: Burkov summarizes an RL paper noting that reward-maximizing post-training collapses model output diversity, which undermines best-of-N search loops that rely on varied candidates checked cheaply, and that real-task rewards are often messier.

items.title:
Turns out giant AI models only need 29 neurons out of 5,632.

Most of an LLM's compute goes into its...
items.content:
Turns out giant AI models only need 29 neurons out of 5,632.

Most of an LLM's compute goes into its feedforward layers. 

These layers hold two-thirds of the parameters and 80% of the math.

The catch is that GPUs are built for dense work. 

Skipping zeros usually gives no real speedup, so sparsity stays theoretical.

This new paper changes that by redesigning the math around how GPUs actually run.

They forced the model to ignore unused neurons during training. 

A simple penalty pushed activity down to 99% sparsity, firing just 29 neurons out of 5632.

Custom GPU kernels then turned that into real wins:

> 21% faster training steps
> 20% faster inference
> 22% less training memory
> 17% lower energy per token

The benefits grow as models get bigger.

All code and kernels release under an open-source license.

Could sparsity become the next big lever for cheaper models?
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI summarizes a paper achieving 99 percent sparsity in feedforward layers with custom GPU kernels yielding faster training, inference, lower memory and energy.
items.reasoning: Concrete, technically detailed research summary with open-source code and meaningful efficiency gains, decent engagement.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI summarizes a paper achieving 99 percent sparsity in feedforward layers with custom GPU kernels yielding faster training, inference, lower memory and energy.

items.title: We talk a lot about how important it is to set up self-verification loops. Especially in the age of ...
items.content:
We talk a lot about how important it is to set up self-verification loops. Especially in the age of powerful models that can run for long periods of time, self-verification is a key ingredient that enables the model to run for much longer, delivering a result that is closer to what you intended, so you can do more without having to constantly check in on Claude as it works.

@delba_oliveira gives a great breakdown of what that looks like and why it matters
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: Cherny emphasizes self-verification loops as a key technique for letting powerful models run longer autonomously, highlighting a colleague's breakdown.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical insight on agentic workflows from Anthropic with strong reach (262k views); broadly applicable guidance.

items.title: I'm really not interested in testing a new closed-weight model in the expensive tier. What's the poi...
items.content:
I'm really not interested in testing a new closed-weight model in the expensive tier. What's the point? I know what will happen:

1. They present the model.

2. The model beats the competition on benchmarks.

3. The users report anecdotal real improvements.

4. The users switch to this model.

5. They start to optimize the model to reduce the inference costs.

6. The users start complaining.

7. The users start hating what the model has become after the optimizations and start looking at the competition.

8. Go to 1.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov outlines a cynical recurring cycle for premium closed-weight models: launch, benchmark wins, user praise, then cost optimization, quality degradation complaints, and churn back to competitors.
items.reasoning: Practitioner offering a sharp, widely-relatable critique of model lifecycle economics; strong engagement and an original framing of the closed-model degradation pattern.
items.summary_html: Burkov outlines a cynical recurring cycle for premium closed-weight models: launch, benchmark wins, user praise, then cost optimization, quality degradation complaints, and churn back to competitors.
items.content_html:
I'm really not interested in testing a new closed-weight model in the expensive tier. What's the point? I know what will happen:

1. They present the model.

2. The model beats the competition on benchmarks.

3. The users report anecdotal real improvements.

4. The users switch to this model.

5. They start to optimize the model to reduce the inference costs.

6. The users start complaining.

7. The users start hating what the model has become after the optimizations and start looking at the competition.

8. Go to 1.

items.title:
You can now train and test world models in one place. 

Researchers kept rebuilding the same machine...
items.content:
You can now train and test world models in one place. 

Researchers kept rebuilding the same machinery for world models.

These systems let agents imagine outcomes before acting.

Every project shipped its own training stack and data loader.

Nothing was comparable.

stable-worldmodel fixes this with one open-source platform for the whole pipeline.

It ships with ready-made baselines like DINO-WM.

Proven planning solvers come built in too.

The data layer streams over 5,000 samples per second, ending the slowdowns that starved GPUs.

Then it runs models through 150+ environments.

You tweak gravity, friction, color, and shapes on demand.

The results expose something uncomfortable:

> Models break under tiny shifts
> Low error rarely means success
>Robust generalization stays unreached

So a model can predict frames almost perfectly and still fail to plan.

What does understanding physics actually require?

items.summary: Alpha Signal AI introduces stable-worldmodel, an open-source platform unifying world-model training and testing with baselines like DINO-WM, fast data streaming, and 150+ environments, noting models break under small shifts and that low prediction error rarely means planning success.

items.title: As frontier models (e.g. Fable 5) continue to push the task horizon of knowledge work automation, it...
items.content:
As frontier models (e.g. Fable 5) continue to push the task horizon of knowledge work automation, it becomes ever more important for humans to be able to audit decisions back to the source context.

It is extremely easy for agents to cite an entire document or document page, but much harder for them to trace back to the exact numbers/words/figures within a page. 

Today we've launched granular bounding boxes within LlamaParse, which allows you to obtain visual citations of every single word in the document. This allows human users to audit exact words and figures - not just general document regions or entire pages! 

Come check it out: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu announces granular bounding boxes in LlamaParse enabling visual citations of every word so humans can audit agent-cited sources.
items.reasoning: Concrete product launch addressing auditability of agent outputs, from a credible founder with strong engagement and clear relevance to knowledge-work automation.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu announces granular bounding boxes in LlamaParse enabling visual citations of every word so humans can audit agent-cited sources.

items.title: 1. Pausing until we had a much firmer idea of how to make AI safe—and well-aligned with human values...
items.content:
1. Pausing until we had a much firmer idea of how to make AI safe—and well-aligned with human values—might well be a good idea.
but
2. I trust neither of these companies, but fear that both will say “what about China”, if push comes to shove, and never actually agree to pause. 

So it comes off to me as pre-IPO marketing, in effort to address public backlash.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus argues that Anthropic and OpenAI statements about pausing AI development are unconvincing, suspecting they will invoke competition with China to avoid acting, and reads it as pre-IPO marketing to address public backlash.
items.reasoning: Recognized critic offering a pointed, skeptical framing of AI lab safety rhetoric; moderate engagement and timely given the simultaneous lab posts about slowing development.
items.summary_html: Marcus argues that Anthropic and OpenAI statements about pausing AI development are unconvincing, suspecting they will invoke competition with China to avoid acting, and reads it as pre-IPO marketing to address public backlash.
items.content_html:
1. Pausing until we had a much firmer idea of how to make AI safe—and well-aligned with human values—might well be a good idea.

but

2. I trust neither of these companies, but fear that both will say “what about China”, if push comes to shove, and never actually agree to pause.

So it comes off to me as pre-IPO marketing, in effort to address public backlash.

Agent filesystems are the new RAG

items.title: Speech translation has been one of the longest-running ML efforts at Google, and we’ve come a long w...
items.content:
Speech translation has been one of the longest-running ML efforts at Google, and we’ve come a long way.  Gemini 3.5 Live Translate is our latest speech-to-speech model, supporting 70+ languages.  It enables more natural conversations across languages in everyday products and apps.

Here’s an example of how partners at @InsideGrab are helping connect travelers with drivers. 🚗

Rolling out in Google Translate and via the Live API in @GoogleAIStudio.

Rolling out in Google Translate and via the Live API in @GoogleAIStudio.

Today, Cohere announced a new partnership with the Government of Québec.

Québec has long been one o...
items.content:
Today, Cohere announced a new partnership with the Government of Québec.

Québec has long been one of the most important AI ecosystems in the world. We’re proud to partner with the Government of Québec to support secure, sovereign, Canadian-built AI that reflects the communities it serves and is deployed with the security and control governments require.

This is an important step in showing how world-class AI can be built in Canada, adopted in Canada, and used to improve the public services people depend on every day.

items.title: For our free newsletter this week, we write about how governments have an interest in investing dire...
items.content:
For our free newsletter this week, we write about how governments have an interest in investing directly in AI.

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
AI is becoming an investment race as governments seek more influence over the companies and infrastructure shaping the future economy. 

The U.S. is debating whether the public should have a stake in major AI companies, while the U.K. and South Korea are already investing directly in AI infrastructure, computing capacity, and chips to strengthen their national competitiveness.

Read and subscribe for free: https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl

items.title:
The pizza dinner test.

We get together as a family for pizza on Sunday nights. Lately, those dinner...
items.content:
The pizza dinner test.

We get together as a family for pizza on Sunday nights. Lately, those dinners have also become a place where I test new AI systems.

This week’s test: @airtap_ai, https://t.co/C6IBaddHul.

Airtap is different from a normal AI assistant. It can operate mobile apps on a phone: tap, scroll, type, wait for the right moment, and move through real app workflows.

The example that made it click for me was timed mobile tasks.

Think restaurant reservations that open at midnight. Gym classes that fill up in seconds. Flight check-ins where seat choice matters. Limited product drops where speed and timing matter.

Instead of sitting there with your phone open, you can set up the workflow from your computer and let Airtap run the phone steps for you.

That is not a normal chatbot task. It is a phone task.

Airtap’s pitch is simple:

Stop running errands for your phone.

More tomorrow on the workflows I’d actually trust it with.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble reviews Airtap, an AI agent that operates mobile apps to handle timed tasks like reservations and limited drops.
items.reasoning: Strong engagement and a substantive look at a novel phone-operating agent category, though framed as a product showcase.

items.title: what does this even mean, @dwarkesh_sp, “the real deal”? is it even a falsifiable conjecture? what’s...
items.content:
what does this even mean, @dwarkesh_sp, “the real deal”? is it even a falsifiable conjecture? what’s the evidence?

and if you can agree that “If you ask an LLM a question it can't answer, sometimes it will just try to imitate reasoning without doing it”, why acknowledge the possibility of imitation when it gets wrong but not when it gets it right?

seems like a double standard?
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus challenges Dwarkesh Patel's phrase the real deal as unfalsifiable, arguing a double standard in attributing reasoning when right but imitation when wrong.
items.reasoning: Substantive epistemic critique central to the reasoning debate with good engagement.
items.summary_html: Marcus challenges Dwarkesh Patel's phrase the real deal as unfalsifiable, arguing a double standard in attributing reasoning when right but imitation when wrong.
items.content_html:
what does this even mean, @dwarkesh_sp, “the real deal”? is it even a falsifiable conjecture? what’s the evidence?

and if you can agree that “If you ask an LLM a question it can't answer, sometimes it will just try to imitate reasoning without doing it”, why acknowledge the possibility of imitation when it gets wrong but not when it gets it right?

seems like a double standard?

items.title:
Small models don't just learn slower.

They're structurally locked out of rare tasks forever.

No ma...
items.content:
Small models don't just learn slower.

They're structurally locked out of rare tasks forever.

No matter how long you train them, frequent task signals constantly overwrite rare ones.

Large models solve this by fully satisfying common tasks first, then dedicating leftover capacity to rare ones.

More data won't fix a capacity problem.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI argues small models are structurally locked out of rare tasks because frequent signals overwrite rare ones, framing it as a capacity not data problem.
items.reasoning: Interesting research-grounded claim about model capacity and rare-task learning, though modest engagement.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI argues small models are structurally locked out of rare tasks because frequent signals overwrite rare ones, framing it as a capacity not data problem.
items.content_html:
Small models don't just learn slower.

They're structurally locked out of rare tasks forever.

No matter how long you train them, frequent task signals constantly overwrite rare ones.

Large models solve this by fully satisfying common tasks first, then dedicating leftover capacity to rare ones.

More data won't fix a capacity problem.

items.title: Today, we released Gemini 3.5 Live Translate, our latest audio model for live speech-to-speech trans...
items.content:
Today, we released Gemini 3.5 Live Translate, our latest audio model for live speech-to-speech translation.

It supports over 70 languages and starts translating as soon as you start talking, streaming translations while listening to what you say next. No awkward pauses or choppy audio, just real connection without language barriers.

So, how does it work? 🤔

The model is able to make split-second decisions to juggle speed and translation quality so conversations actually feel fluid, human, and natural. In order to do this, the model must receive and contextualize the input while simultaneously outputting the translated speech.

Through this process, Gemini 3.5 Live Translate manages to stay mere seconds behind each speaker and can even maintain pacing, pitch, and intonation across extended sessions.

See it in action below, or try it yourself in the Google Translate app on iOS & Android.

So, how does it work? 🤔

items.title: me January 2025 @politico, timing slightly off: we may soon see “the largest cyberattack in history,...
items.content:
me January 2025 @politico, timing slightly off: we may soon see “the largest cyberattack in history, taking down, at least for a little while, some sizeable piece of the world’s infrastructure… generative AI tools are increasingly being used to create code; in some cases those coders don’t fully understand the code written, and the autogenerated code has already been shown in some cases to introduce new security holes  … the world [is] quite vulnerable to ever more audacious attacks.”

meta, right about now: oh shit, he might have a point.

items.title: 3⃣ Source control wasn't built for this. When a human and an LLM co-author the same PR, line-level d...
items.content: 3⃣ Source control wasn't built for this. When a human and an LLM co-author the same PR, line-level diffs don't cut it - he wants character-level provenance, partly so a regulated shop can answer "what did this agent do at 2 pm three years ago."

items.title: When I worked at Microsoft in the 2000s I used its business intelligence tools to answer questions, ...
items.content:
When I worked at Microsoft in the 2000s I used its business intelligence tools to answer questions, so I could blog about them like these.

Damn, I wish I had this back then. 

Every AI analytics tool asks you to just trust the number.

Mora shows you the SQL it wrote against your own schema, so you can verify every answer yourself.

Ask a hard revenue question in plain English. Get a verified breakdown in seconds. Then tell it to build the dashboard.

Start asking hard questions at https://t.co/Wh8vLQpHXs @mora_HQ

items.title:
This is so freaking cool!

When I'm in Spain, I never go to the movie theater if they only play dubb...
items.content:
This is so freaking cool!

When I'm in Spain, I never go to the movie theater if they only play dubbed movies. Horrible experience watching Hollywood actors speak with a Spanish accent in voices that aren't theirs.

This will make all of that go away.

By the way,  I just got back from Japan, and I couldn't understand anyone because Japanese is hard, and I don't know any of it.

And Google Translate sucks, honestly.

So please, somebody, focus on this for real-time conversations!

items.title:
This is not the first tool to promise AI-backed analytics.

I've tried a ton, some better than other...
items.content:
This is not the first tool to promise AI-backed analytics.

I've tried a ton, some better than others, but most suffer from hallucinations.

There are 3 things these guys are doing that I like:

1. Showing you the SQL they ran to give you the results. Assuming you can read SQL, you can verify you are getting the correct result.

2. The speed is pretty nuts. Getting instant results regardless of how many rows you need to retrieve is cool.

3. MCP lets you run this everywhere, which opens a ton of use cases.

We'll see how this plays out.

items.title: Trained on the new & existing data, ACE2S-SHiELD+ accurately handles the scenarios earlier ACE m...
items.content:
Trained on the new & existing data, ACE2S-SHiELD+ accurately handles the scenarios earlier ACE models were good at as well as the ones they struggled with.

It's more flexible than ACE2-SHiELD + ACE2-SOM combined, using ~25% fewer training samples than either alone. https://t.co/uFPq1TdlD0

items.title:
We've been developing the ACE family for several years. 

ACE2-SHiELD was trained on historical simu...
items.content:
We've been developing the ACE family for several years. 

ACE2-SHiELD was trained on historical simulations from a physics-based model. ACE2-SOM came next, coupling ACE2 to a slab ocean—a simplified ocean representation where ocean temperature responds to CO2.
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: Recaps the multi-year ACE emulator lineage, from ACE2-SHiELD trained on physics simulations to ACE2-SOM with a slab ocean.
items.reasoning: Provides helpful research provenance from a credible lab, though it remains a contextual thread tweet.
items.summary_html: Recaps the multi-year ACE emulator lineage, from ACE2-SHiELD trained on physics simulations to ACE2-SOM with a slab ocean.
items.content_html:
We've been developing the ACE family for several years.

ACE2-SHiELD was trained on historical simulations from a physics-based model. ACE2-SOM came next, coupling ACE2 to a slab ocean—a simplified ocean representation where ocean temperature responds to CO2.

items.content_html:
@omooretweets Yeah, none of my family and friends have even asked me about a single model. There is a huge gap between X and everybody else.

The technical world has coalesced around here, which I like a lot. But it's on my mind: how do you reach normal people? How do you get them to care about this stuff?

It's probably not going to happen. They're going to stick with Siri.

Le Québec est ...
items.content:
Aujourd'hui, Cohere a annoncé un nouveau partenariat avec le gouvernement du Québec.

Le Québec est depuis longtemps l'un des écosystèmes d'IA les plus importants au monde. Nous sommes fiers de collaborer avec le gouvernement du Québec pour soutenir une IA sécurisée, souveraine et construite au Canada, qui reflète les communautés qu'elle sert et qui est déployée avec la sécurité et le contrôle requis par les gouvernements.

C'est une étape importante pour montrer comment une IA de classe mondiale peut être construite au Canada, adoptée au Canada et utilisée pour améliorer les services publics dont les citoyens dépendent chaque jour.

items.title:
The Hundred-Page Machine Learning Book by Andriy Burkov is widely regarded as a good book.

Ruthless...
items.content:
The Hundred-Page Machine Learning Book by Andriy Burkov is widely regarded as a good book.

Ruthless signal-to-noise ratio. Burkov made a deliberate editorial choice to include only what matters and cut everything else. There's no padding, no filler anecdotes, no 30-page warm-ups before the real content. Every paragraph is doing work. This respects your time in a way most textbooks don't, and it means you can actually finish it—unlike the 800-page tomes that sit half-read on shelves.

It gives you the map before the territory. The book's real value is structural: it shows how the pieces of ML fit together as a coherent whole. You come away understanding where SVMs sit relative to logistic regression, how regularization connects to the bias-variance tradeoff, why gradient descent underlies so much of the field. That scaffolding is exactly what's hard to build when you learn topics piecemeal from scattered tutorials, and it's what makes everything you learn afterward stick better.

The math is present but proportionate. Burkov includes the linear algebra, probability, and calculus you actually need to understand the algorithms—derivations of gradient descent, the intuition behind kernels, the probabilistic reasoning under Naive Bayes—without drowning you in proofs. It's enough to make the methods feel principled rather than magical, but not so much that the math becomes the obstacle.

Practitioner-grade priorities. The book spends real estate on things working ML people care about: feature engineering, handling imbalanced data, hyperparameter tuning, regularization, model evaluation and metrics, the difference between what works in a paper and what works in production. This is hard-won practical judgment, not just textbook theory, and it's the part that's genuinely useful when you sit down to build something.

Writing quality and credibility. Burkov is a clear, economical writer, and the prose reflects someone who deeply understands the material—you only achieve this kind of compression when you truly grasp a subject. The endorsements from people like Peter Norvig, and its adoption as a quick reference by working engineers and as interview-prep across the industry, reflect that the compression didn't cost it correctness or usefulness.

Exceptional value and accessibility. The "read first, buy later" model lets you evaluate the entire book before paying anything, which is rare and signals real confidence. For the price and time investment, the return—a working conceptual command of the entire ML field—is among the best available.

Versatile across use cases. It works as a first orientation, a refresher when you're returning to a topic, a quick reference to look something up, and a structured review before interviews. Few books serve that many roles well.

#LMtrainingData

items.title: Today we're excited to introduce vime — a simple, stable, and efficient RL framework for LLM post-tr...
items.content:
Today we're excited to introduce vime — a simple, stable, and efficient RL framework for LLM post-training in the vLLM ecosystem.

Built on slime's proven training design and powered by vLLM inference, vime brings another strong option to the growing vLLM post-training ecosystem.

Our goal isn't a one-size-fits-all framework. We want users with different needs to find the right vLLM-ecosystem choice for their workflows—whether that's vime, NeMo RL, OpenRLHF, verl, or others.

More choice. More interoperability. More innovation.

Learn more: https://t.co/c3yfEewsWj

#LLM #RLHF #PostTraining #vLLM

Mythos is live! so excited to have our FrontierCode recognized as the next frontier coding bench.

on FC Diamond, BOTH Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.5 don't meaningfully scale with effort, which many of you caught yesterday.

Mythos/Fable posttraining have really applied that test time compute toward solving very, very long running problems - dozens of human hour equivalents, hundreds of dollars per task, for the first time ever measured.

Available now in @Cognition @Devin for only 1.4x ACUs too! (I never thought i'd see this launch lol)

"It is state-of-the-art on nearly all tested benchmarks of AI capability, showing exceptional performance in software engineering, knowledge work, vision, scientific research, and many other areas."

Claude Mythos 5 is also launching to a "small group of cyberdefenders and infrastructure providers", which has less safeguards than Fable.

items.title: Earlier ACE models produced unrealistic results on two scenarios climate scientists often use to pro...
items.content: Earlier ACE models produced unrealistic results on two scenarios climate scientists often use to probe model behavior: AMIP +4 K, which raises sea surface temperature by 4 degrees with CO2 unchanged, and abrupt 4xCO2, which quadruples CO2 against a still-cold ocean.
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: Notes earlier ACE models failed on AMIP +4K and abrupt 4xCO2 scenarios used to probe climate model behavior.
items.reasoning: Domain-specific context on prior model limitations, but minimal reach and incremental within the thread.
items.summary_html: Notes earlier ACE models failed on AMIP +4K and abrupt 4xCO2 scenarios used to probe climate model behavior.
items.content_html: Earlier ACE models produced unrealistic results on two scenarios climate scientists often use to probe model behavior: AMIP +4 K, which raises sea surface temperature by 4 degrees with CO2 unchanged, and abrupt 4xCO2, which quadruples CO2 against a still-cold ocean.

items.title: Sea surface temperature & CO2 have major impacts on climate, and they typically change together—...
items.content:
Sea surface temperature & CO2 have major impacts on climate, and they typically change together—SST tends to rise as CO2 increases. 

Because earlier ACE models had only seen them move in sync, they couldn't accurately predict what happens when one changes and the other doesn't.
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: Explains that sea surface temperature and CO2 normally move together, so earlier models could not predict outcomes when only one changes.
items.reasoning: Clear scientific framing of the problem ACE2S addresses, but low-engagement thread content.
items.summary_html: Explains that sea surface temperature and CO2 normally move together, so earlier models could not predict outcomes when only one changes.
items.content_html:
Sea surface temperature & CO2 have major impacts on climate, and they typically change together—SST tends to rise as CO2 increases.

Because earlier ACE models had only seen them move in sync, they couldn't accurately predict what happens when one changes and the other doesn't.

items.title: XGBoost for Regression, Predictive Modeling, and Time Series Analysis — Learn how to build, evaluate...
items.content:
XGBoost for Regression, Predictive Modeling, and Time Series Analysis — Learn how to build, evaluate, & deploy predictive models:
https://t.co/DsGwnEXyWF v/ @PacktDataML
—
My review:

🟠XGBoost is definitely the focal point and central contribution of this book, along with all those other things: Regression Trees, Predictive Modeling, Time Series Analysis, and Forecasting. 

🟠This book’s added value comes in its thorough progression from start to finish with comprehensive coverage of all the major steps and issues in data science and machine learning modeling: data cleaning, imbalanced data, feature engineering, feature engineering pipeline, feature importance, categorical data encoding, model interpretability and explainability, ensembles, model training, model evaluation metrics, hyperparameter tuning, model deployment, and more - including a quick-start case study starter project that feeds into next-level projects. 

🟠I highly recommend this broadly useful book for all levels of practitioners.
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne reviews an XGBoost book covering regression, time series, and the full data science modeling pipeline.
items.reasoning: Detailed educational review from a known data science figure but it is essentially a book promotion with modest engagement.

items.title: The enterprise question coming out of the PGA Championship is not “what technology did T-Mobile depl...
items.content:
The enterprise question coming out of the PGA Championship is not “what technology did T-Mobile deploy?”

It is: is your network built to support multiple systems simultaneously, under pressure, at scale?

Because the network is no longer background infrastructure.

It is part of the operational infrastructure behind the experience.

I broke down what that looked like across broadcast, operations, fan experience, and workforce in my latest video with @TMobileBusiness.

Watch it here: https://t.co/8l1WLRTZ5A

What would it take for your organization’s network to support this kind of operational demand? Drop your thoughts below.

T-Mobile for Business Partner

items.title:
Then there are the layers most people overlook.

→ 5G Transact supporting payments across the venue
...
items.content:
Then there are the layers most people overlook.

→ 5G Transact supporting payments across the venue
→ SuperMobile helping keep the workforce connected
→ The AI Assistant in the PGA app supporting fan questions

Different systems. Different users. One connected operational environment.

This is not about any single technology. It is about what becomes possible when multiple systems operate on network infrastructure designed to support concurrent demand.

Network slicing means dedicated capacity for AI computer vision, simultaneously supporting tens of thousands of fan devices, without one system slowing down another.

That is enterprise infrastructure design in practice. And it is a direct CIO conversation.

items.title: Most people think the network is background infrastructure at a live sports event. The PGA Champions...
items.content:
Most people think the network is background infrastructure at a live sports event. The PGA Championship just proved that thinking is already obsolete.

As a T-Mobile for Business Partner, I was on site across 300+ acres where broadcast, operations, fan experience, workforce, and commerce all had to run simultaneously across a highly demanding operational environment.

What I saw changed how I think about enterprise infrastructure entirely.

A thread on how connected event infrastructure is evolving

items.title:
Network slicing isn't a consumer feature.

It's how you give dedicated bandwidth to mission-critical...
items.content:
Network slicing isn't a consumer feature.

It's how you give dedicated bandwidth to mission-critical workloads, without letting everything else compete for the same pipe.

At the PGA Championship, that looked like this:

→ AI computer vision for 60 cameras, dedicated throughput → Fan devices getting full connectivity, simultaneously → POS systems at concessions, ticketing at every gate, all on the same network → None of them slowing each other down

300+ acres. One network. Every system performing.

That's the proof point.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.content_html:
Network slicing isn't a consumer feature.

It's how you give dedicated bandwidth to mission-critical workloads, without letting everything else compete for the same pipe.

At the PGA Championship, that looked like this:

→ AI computer vision for 60 cameras, dedicated throughput → Fan devices getting full connectivity, simultaneously → POS systems at concessions, ticketing at every gate, all on the same network → None of them slowing each other down

300+ acres. One network. Every system performing.

That's the proof point.

items.title:
Now zoom out to operations.

Running a 300-acre event means managing crowd movement, entry points, c...
items.content:
Now zoom out to operations.

Running a 300-acre event means managing crowd movement, entry points, concessions, connected devices, and staff coordination simultaneously.

The Operations Command Center brought that fragmented operational data into real-time operational visibility.

Powered by T-Mobile’s 5G network, the platform supported operational workflows across the venue.

Powered by CapTech’s AI, patterns surfaced earlier in the process.

Network infrastructure plays a major role in how AI performs at scale.

items.title: Let’s start with broadcast, because this is where the shift is most visible. The T-Mobile Range Show...
items.content:
Let’s start with broadcast, because this is where the shift is most visible. The T-Mobile Range Show is not a demo. It is a live production environment.

What it runs on:

→ 5G On Demand powering roaming cameras across the course → A mobile 4D capture system delivering broadcast-quality content without permanent infrastructure → 360 degree swing analysis streamed in real time

The network is not supporting the broadcast.
The network became part of the production infrastructure.

items.title:
Stadiums. Hospitals. Campuses.

The architecture problem is the same everywhere: how do you run AI a...
items.content:
Stadiums. Hospitals. Campuses.

The architecture problem is the same everywhere: how do you run AI and thousands of simultaneous connections without one system slowing down another?

The PGA Championship just ran that experiment live, in front of the world.

Watch the full breakdown of the 5G architecture powering it here: https://t.co/8l1WLRTZ5A

Thanks to @TMobileBusiness for the behind-the-scenes access. More coming from this series.

Your AI strategy is only as good as the network running it. What does yours look like when everything hits at once?

T-Mobile for Business Partner

One network—without one system slowing down another.

The reason? Network slicing.

items.title:
@georgecursor So many things to reply to this

But the main thing is that you believe entrepreneursh...
items.content:
@georgecursor So many things to reply to this

But the main thing is that you believe entrepreneurship is a game where everybody can win

Well you kinda can, but generally like literally most pursuits it's a power law of success, most people don't make it, some do great, many do well

Any skill based pursuit is like that, sports, music, even regular careers

It feels what you're looking for is a world of participation trophies where everybody wins all the time

That's not a natural world
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio argues entrepreneurship follows a power law where most do not make it, rejecting a participation-trophy worldview.
items.reasoning: Engaging entrepreneurship debate but off-topic for AI/ML.

That's not a natural world

items.title:

Built with PGA of America and CapTech, it g...
items.content:
At the center of it all: the Operations Command Center.

Built with PGA of America and CapTech, it gave operators a single live view across the entire venue.

Crowd movement. Entry points. Concessions. Connected devices. All feeding into one place, in real time.

Powered by T-Mobile's 5G network handling data transmission at the speed AI inference requires. Powered by CapTech's AI surfacing issues as they emerge.

This is what AI at scale actually looks like when the network underneath it can handle the load.

items.title:
The fan experience ran on that same infrastructure.

Seat Finder showing real-time seat availability...
items.content:
The fan experience ran on that same infrastructure.

Seat Finder showing real-time seat availability, AI-driven. Roar Moments surfacing highlights the second they happen. The AI Assistant in the PGA app answering questions.

None of that works if the network underneath it can't carry the load at scale.

T-Mobile's 5G network is the backbone. CapTech's AI is what turns real-time data into real-time decisions.

That combination is the enterprise insight.

It is a lifesaver, and I'm so excited more and more people are finally figuring it out and getting into the FSD tribe.

items.content_html:
@KinvertOG @TroyJackson207 In California we had a serial killer decades ago that caused havoc and many tragedies. He caused the state to invest in many things to catch him, including the 9-1-1 system we all use today.

Technology catches criminals.

To not use it is stupid. And communities that remove them see increases in crime and handcuff police from a major tool.

This is why it solves crimes and, even, prevents them in communities that have installed them.

items.title:
My 2025 Tesla Y is a great car

I never had a car before so don't trust me but I did ride a lot of T...
items.content:
My 2025 Tesla Y is a great car

I never had a car before so don't trust me but I did ride a lot of Teslas before in Uber, specifically Tesla 3 and I didn't like it cause it felt cheap and shaky and I'd get car sick (I never get car sick)

Not the 2025 Tesla Y, even as a passenger I don't get car sick, it feels smooth, it doesn't feel cheap

Most of all my friends with petrol cars spend a lot of time in garages, meanwhile we've never

Only when it delivered, it showed an error that the cameras did not calibrate, we brought it to the garage and a cable was loose during production and they fixed it and since then it just works

I like driving it because I know the safety score is good so if something happens there's good odds we survive it as it's built well

Only thing I don't like is that sometimes it does automatic phantom breaking on the highway in Portugal, that's extremely dangerous because if someone is behind you, they might hit you, I think it's because in EU we still run on the old software and not FSD yet (except NL recently), also you do need to clean the cameras regularly but also there's lots of construction dust near us so that doesn't help

So yeah I like it a lot, it's a car that's not annoying, never breaks, doesn't need maintenance
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio reviews his 2025 Tesla Y favorably while noting phantom braking dangers on EU highways.
items.reasoning: High-engagement but off-topic personal car review with no AI/ML content.

items.title:
@KinvertOG @TroyJackson207 I once met the former police commissioner for New York City. 

"I saw you...
items.content:
@KinvertOG @TroyJackson207 I once met the former police commissioner for New York City. 

"I saw your worst day on TV, that was 9/11, what was your best day?"

"I solved 300 crimes with the stroke of my pen."

"How?"

"I wrote a check for 12 million to have all our rape kits digitized."

There's a ton of crime that goes un prosecuted. But you changed the argument. 

This isn't about NOT doing more with courts. 

It's about catching criminals in the act. 

Whether you prosecute them or not is a different thing.

items.content:
@1under4 @TroyJackson207 Yeah, except we haven't had another 9/11 yet have we?

The reality is that there are people who will do you harm. 

I visited San Quentin Prison where everyone killed someone else to get in there. 

There was a building even they didn't want to go in with the most dangerous of the dangerous. 

It's better we catch them fast than stay in the past.
